title: The Effects of U.S. Democratic Backsliding on U.S. Power
gist: Carnegie's Thomas Carothers argues that U.S. democratic backsliding under Trump-era polarization and 'executive aggrandizement' is measurably eroding four distinct components of U.S. power — attractiveness, reliability, effectiveness, and focus — with effects (hollowed alliances, hollowed bureaucracy, policy lurching, self-dealing) that are largely structural and slow to reverse even if the political trajectory changes.

themes.theme: The mechanics of backsliding: polarization plus executive aggrandizement

insights.claim: U.S. political polarization has hardened since the 1990s into two camps that no longer share consensus on basic political norms, driven by rising socioeconomic inequality, partisan media (talk radio, cable, social media), and a surge of ideologically motivated high-net-worth political donors on both sides.
insights.speaker: Thomas Carothers
insights.so_what: Identifies the structural drivers of US polarization as durable (media economics, campaign finance) rather than tied to any single administration, implying the underlying condition outlasts Trump.
insights.locator: Various broader factors have contributed to this intensification of polarization

insights.claim: Trump's project goes beyond ordinary polarization into 'executive aggrandizement': asserting presidential dominance over nonpartisan executive institutions (independent agencies, military, civil service), sidelining Congress and the courts, magnifying federal power over states, and punishing nongovernmental critics (media, law firms, universities, civic groups).
insights.speaker: Thomas Carothers
insights.so_what: A named, three-part analytical framework for tracking the institutional erosion — useful as a checklist for monitoring further consolidation moves (agency capture, court defiance, state-authority preemption, NGO/media targeting).
insights.locator: The ongoing executive aggrandizement has three core, interlocking components

insights.claim: The Trump administration reinforces executive aggrandizement by blurring the distinction between Trump personally and the presidency itself — branding initiatives with his name, circulating hagiographic AI-generated images of him, and messaging around his 'unique' abilities — so that criticism of Trump reads as an attack on the office.
insights.speaker: Thomas Carothers
insights.so_what: A propaganda mechanism (personality-cult branding) that entrenches power beyond formal institutional capture — a soft-power/domestic-narrative risk factor for anyone assessing US governance stability.
insights.locator: Trump and his team continuously wrap all three of these elements

themes.theme: Attractiveness: alliances and rule of law erode

insights.claim: U.S. soft power has historically rested on being a democratic exemplar, which built deeper and more stable alliances with European and East Asian democracies than with nondemocratic powers; that leadership position is now eroding as the administration openly disparages values-based alliance ties.
insights.speaker: Thomas Carothers
insights.so_what: Alliance cohesion — a key US structural advantage over China/Russia — is the primary channel through which democratic backsliding degrades hard geopolitical power, not just reputation.
insights.quote: the bedrock of a durable partnership is not based on idealistic values.
insights.locator: allies took note when Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth said

insights.claim: Trump has actively intervened to sway foreign elections — publicly endorsing the far-right candidate in Honduras's 2025 presidential election and offering Argentina $20 billion in 2025 conditional on the right-wing party winning legislative elections — positioning the administration as working against electoral integrity abroad rather than supporting it.
insights.speaker: Thomas Carothers
insights.so_what: A concrete pattern of transactional election interference that undercuts any residual US claim to democracy-promotion credibility, with second-order effects on how much other governments trust US-backed political outcomes.
insights.locator: Trump’s frequent efforts to sway the outcomes of elections

insights.claim: The administration's disregard for the rule of law — arbitrary application of immigration law and personal-preference overrides of legal norms in business dealings — reduces U.S. attractiveness for both foreign capital and skilled foreign talent, historically a major driver of U.S. tech-sector strength.
insights.speaker: Thomas Carothers
insights.so_what: A direct channel from rule-of-law erosion to reduced talent/capital inflows — a headwind for the US tech and innovation base that compounds AI-race competitiveness concerns.
insights.locator: The administration’s pointed disregard or lack of respect for the rule of law

themes.theme: Reliability: policy lurching burns trust with allies and adversaries

insights.claim: Severe polarization causes U.S. foreign policy to 'lurch' between administrations well beyond normal democratic alternation, making allies see the U.S. as less reliable — Canada, Germany, and the UK have already de-emphasized ties with Washington in both security and economic domains.
insights.speaker: Thomas Carothers
insights.so_what: Cites Rachel Myrick's research linking domestic polarization directly to alliance de-emphasis — a testable, ongoing trend to monitor in allied defense and trade posture toward the US.
insights.locator: As Rachel Myrick and other scholars have documented

insights.claim: Policy lurching also makes adversaries see the U.S. as an untrustworthy negotiating partner, illustrated by the JCPOA cycle with Iran — signed under Obama, shredded by Trump in his first term, revival attempts under Biden, then open military conflict under Trump's second term, then renewed talks — teaching adversaries that any deal risks being discarded by the next administration.
insights.speaker: Thomas Carothers
insights.so_what: A concrete mechanism raising the cost of any future US-brokered deal (Iran nuclear, or by extension any adversary negotiation) — counterparties will demand steeper terms or refuse durable commitments given the precedent of reversal.
insights.locator: The back-and-forth on U.S. policy toward Iran

themes.theme: Effectiveness: hollowed bureaucracy and sidelined checks

insights.claim: The administration has cut the National Security Council staff by approximately 80%, left over one hundred ambassadorial posts vacant more than a year in, and eliminated USAID, substantially reducing the expertise available to senior foreign-policy makers.
insights.speaker: Thomas Carothers
insights.so_what: Quantifies the policymaking-capacity drawdown — a leading indicator for degraded quality of future US crisis decisions, as already evidenced by the Iran strikes below.
insights.locator: the hollowing out of core foreign policy bureaucratic capacities

insights.claim: The February 2026 U.S. military strikes on Iran appear to have been poorly informed, proceeding without prior in-depth White House analysis of how Iran might respond (including closing the Strait of Hormuz) or how to counter that response, and the post-strike negotiations were not well prepared by experienced experts.
insights.speaker: Thomas Carothers
insights.so_what: A live case study connecting bureaucratic hollowing directly to a specific, high-stakes foreign-policy failure — reinforces that the capacity erosion isn't abstract, it's already shaping outcomes like the Hormuz disruption referenced across Carnegie's other 2026 pieces.
insights.locator: Some of these effects are evident in the administration’s conduct

insights.claim: The abrupt closure of USAID, undertaken without congressional consultation despite Congress's long-standing oversight role, was carried out with no serious review of the reputational and relational damage it would cause, whereas a Congress-included restructuring could have reduced the damage while still delivering reform many agreed was needed.
insights.speaker: Thomas Carothers
insights.so_what: Illustrates that sidelining Congress isn't just a democratic-norms issue but a policy-quality issue — bypassing institutional checks produced a worse outcome even by the administration's own stated reform goals.
insights.locator: The abrupt, hasty closure of USAID

insights.claim: After the Supreme Court ruled in April 2026 against the president's use of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act to justify many tariffs, Trump attacked the justices and moved to work around the ruling rather than comply, even though mainstream economists viewed the tariffs as a net negative for the U.S. economy.
insights.speaker: Thomas Carothers
insights.so_what: A concrete instance where resisting judicial input produced worse economic policy by mainstream consensus — evidence that check-circumvention has direct economic costs, relevant to tariff and trade-policy risk assessment.
insights.locator: After the Supreme Court ruled in April 2026

themes.theme: Focus: power diverted to private gain

insights.claim: Citing a Brennan Center for Justice report, Carothers notes Trump is 'merging the powers of his presidency with his businesses and personal finances to an extent never before seen' — pardoning donors, dropping investigations into benefactors, promoting his family's cryptocurrency and real estate holdings via the presidency, and granting perks to donor companies.
insights.speaker: Thomas Carothers
insights.so_what: Direct evidence of a corruption channel through which foreign governments (per the next insight, Gulf states) can buy access to US foreign policy — a due-diligence flag for entities transacting with Trump-linked business interests.
insights.quote: Trump is merging the powers of his presidency with his businesses and personal finances to an extent never before seen
insights.locator: as a Brennan Center for Justice report notes

insights.claim: Trump family real estate and crypto deals with Gulf countries function as a two-way channel — financially remunerative for the Trump family while giving Gulf governments a path to curry political favor with the White House to advance their own private interests rather than U.S. strategic interests in the region.
insights.speaker: Thomas Carothers
insights.so_what: Names a specific, ongoing conflict-of-interest vector (Gulf-Trump business ties) that likely distorts US MENA policy away from national-interest calculus — relevant to assessing the credibility of any US Gulf security or investment commitments.
insights.locator: The ties of Trump and his family with some of the Gulf countries

themes.theme: Outlook: structural, slow to reverse, and U.S.-specific

insights.claim: Unlike most countries, the U.S. built major elements of its post-WWII power directly around its status as a successful democracy and leader of the free world, making it unusually exposed to power loss from democratic backsliding; autocracies like China might instead see their power fall during a democratic transition.
insights.speaker: Thomas Carothers
insights.so_what: Explains why the democracy-power link found here is not a universal rule but a US-specific vulnerability — meaning the erosion in US relative power vis-à-vis autocratic rivals is structurally asymmetric, not just cyclical.
insights.locator: The United States is unusual in the extent to which significant elements of its power

insights.claim: Even if executive centralization is substantially reversed, the author expects the underlying polarization to persist because its roots run deeper than the current administration, meaning policy lurching and reduced allied trust are likely to continue for an extended period regardless of near-term political change.
insights.speaker: Thomas Carothers
insights.so_what: Sets a realistic timeline expectation: don't underwrite a thesis of quick US alliance-credibility recovery even under a post-Trump administration — rebuilding attractiveness and bureaucratic capacity is described as 'very slow.'
insights.locator: Renewing the attractiveness of U.S. democracy as a political exemplar
